sentences.text: Yeah, actually, December '45.
December '45.
Was second lieutenant.
And I was discharged in Dix.
And we had a chance to enlist in the reserve.
I, through interrogation and talking with German prisoners and other refugees, knew there will be another war with Russia.
It turned out it'd be Korea instead.
But I decided the next time I go into the-- to war, I'm not going towards the front line.
I want to reserve a desk.
So I stayed   in the reserve.
For 20 years.
I retired from the reserve in 19-- 60-- '73.
Lieutenant colonel.
I got the bronze star, as I mentioned, at the end of the war.
And when I retired from the reserve, I was rewarded-- awarded the Army Commendation Medal.
Of course, I have four-- five battle stars.
I went back to my old job.
And as soon as possible, I wanted to go to college.
I used the record, which I fraudulently obtained from the Newark School of Engineering, to go-- get into Stevens Institute of Technology.
And I stayed there for four years, became an engineer-- married in between.
Hilde.
Kaufman.
We were married in January of '48.
Oh, we were introduced by a mutual friend.
Not much.
For most people, either they heard so much about it-- they were sick of it; or they were not interested.
And in between, there were-- there was nothing in between.
Yes, we have two children.
They are--
--Thomas and Barbara.
And they are married.
And each one has two boys.
From our daughter-- whose children-- the older is Leon and Alan.
And our son's children are Richard and Matthew.
Wise.
Barbara.
Nope-- daughter-in-law, oh, sorry.
Our daughter-in-law is Jane.
Her-- her last name is Hagen.
Wise-- Harold Wise.
Most of the time I worked at Bell Laboratories, in Whippany.
But I-- before I had a few other jobs.
Nothing.
And-- and--
Yes, I'm very successful at it.
For most of the years we lived in New Jersey and around New York-- the suburbs.
About 2 and 1/2 years ago when our son got a job here.
And we decided-- I like warmer climate.
I didn't accomplish much, but actually, I am more proud of my military records than of my civilian record-- if that is an indication.
Because in my civilian life, I was always handicapped.
I had, number one, an accent.
And as a professional, I was 10 years older than the average man of my qualification and experience.
And that always held me back.
Yes.
Yes, about my feeling about all of that.
We hear a lot about the Holocaust and how bad it was, for it's the truth, which it definitely was.
I certainly did not want-- do not-- do not want to distract from any of the sufferings-- which I realize is great-- but we were not the only one who suffered.
Let me tell the story of our trustee from Dachau.
His name was Elloris Stangle.
Stangle-- S-T-A-N-G-L-E.
Elloris-- Elloris.
And he was a simple-- barge-- had on the Danube.
And one day, he got drunk-- and in a bar, was very uncomplimentary towards Hitler.
And he had a brother-in-law.
His-- his-- his sister's husband was a ardent Nazi.
So he wound up in Dachau.
And that was four years before, and he was still there.
There was a brief period where they discovered crab lice in our barrack.
Crab lice-- scabies.
And then we were insulated-- or isolated-- for two days, three days.
And we got more personal.
And I talked to him, and we complained bitterly about our fate, which it wasn't anything of pleasure.
He said, what are you talking about?
You are here for six weeks, three months.
I have been here for four weeks.
From those who came six weeks ago, 1/2 of them are already released.
I have not a chance of getting out of here alive, but I am sure my brother-in-law will see to it that he will never see me.
So what-- so it was not only the Jews who suffered.
And he told us a little bit about the history of Dachau.
Dachau, as I mentioned before, was founded in 1933, right away.
And in 1936, it was rebuilt for the primary purpose of taking care of the falloff from the Reichstag.
And from 1936 till 1938, to the Kristallnacht, there were no Jews in Dachau.
They were all political prisoners-- Germans-- fellow Germans.
And so it wasn't only the Jews who suffered.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, I know there was latent anti-- anti-Semitism in Germany.
It was all over Europe.
It's all over the world Yes, I know Hitler got into power with a majority of votes.
But in order for Hitler to do what he wanted to do, a majority wasn't good enough.
He had to get total support, total allegiance, and everybody had to march in the same step.
And that is primarily what the concentration camps were for, and were used for that.
We were just a-- a byproduct.

And one of the biggest crimes in Germany was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This-- this crazy-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- disparaging-- it's a kind of-- it was a political crime punished-- punishable by a concentration camp, which was outside the regular jurisdiction of the German government.
They had each a traditional system which would take care of all the criminals, whatever is there, and then, of course, illegal things.
And here comes the concentration camps, where outside their jurisdiction they could do what they pleased.
And if they didn't like what you said, you got there.
Now, it's a big difference between the Jews and the others, where the Jews were released.
I was released under the conditions that I leave Germany in six months.
And when-- once I was out there, I could tell as much as I wanted about if they listened.
So-- and the rest of the world, including the United States, they say, oh, it's only Jews-- who cares?
And that was the impressions they could find.
However, the Germans in the concentration camp, when any family member was put in the concentration camp, the family had to be super Nazis.
They had to be very careful not to talk to anybody who might be politically not correct.
Their children couldn't talk about their parents, and they could not be seen next to a Jewish kid in the school or anything.
I think there was one fellow.
I know there was one fellow in the-- in class.
His father was sent to Dachau-- to concentration-- but he was away.
He-- I don't know what was-- he was away.
He was particularly careful to stay away from me.
He wouldn't come to me in sympathy.
And-- so-- all the-- and there were strict censorship of mail, of telephones, whatever it is.
The Germans, if you had a family member in the concentration camp, they could not write abroad about that somebody was there.
It was strictly forbidden.
And if finally, the Nazis were ready to release him, they said, now, you will be released under one condition-- that you do not talk about it.
As soon as you talk about it, you come right back here, and you won't get out.
So it was completely hushed up that we are Germans.
And as I said before, the impression created was the impression they wanted to create, and that was a screen with which they could prepare for the war without too much interference from the ally.
You had a German Bund.
You had, in the United States, you had similar organizations all over the world, who tacitly approved of what Germany was doing, who influenced-- influenced the government in the war.
One of the greatest German supporters was Joseph P.
Kennedy.
As this is known.
And he wasn't the only one.

But Hitler wanted to create that impression that everything else within Germany is fine, and it's only the Jews, the vagrants, homosexuals-- there's others-- and to-- who keeps it-- to keeps the streets clean.
Nobody really knew the-- it was-- the extent of the Jews in the concentration camp wasn't known.
But less known, much less known, was the extent to really the way the Germans were treated there.
I said, Dachau was for-- for two years without a single Jew-- just as an example.
And when the war broke out, nobody cared anymore what went out, or that nothing went out anyway.
Hitler had achieved what he wanted to achieve.
And he used the Jews.
He used homosexuals, the gypsies, whatever it is, as a cover.
We were-- and as such, we were very-- he was very successful.
And that shouldn't be overlook.
Once Hitler came to-- into power, to keep such a tight lid on his own people, he needed lots of people.
There was-- it wasn't just a few people on top who said, do that and that.
There were hordes of henchmen who had to be there-- volunteers.
And the biggest shame of the Germans is that Germany was able to have so many henchmen.
And even nowadays, they don't talk about it.
For everybody in Germany, whatever they thought, to some extent were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --involved.
I-- I can't think of the word.
And so that's interesting.
So they don't-- nobody wants to talk about it.
They couldn't talk about it before.
They couldn't talk about it afterwards, because their neighbors also lived in-- lived-- they had to still live with neighbors.
They didn't know who was who.
So it became-- it was hushed up.
And that is probably the last one-- is the basis on which some claim that the Holocaust never existed, because you hear the stories only from non-Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that has to be considered, and it's very easy to say that the Germans who set out all, or not all, or whatever it is.
The biggest crime, as I see, is what the Germans did with themself.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because it was-- not worse.
We did suffer.
I think it was-- for a German-- it was more insidious.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And actually, that is a message we should portray, I think.
That's the way it really was inside Germany.
It was-- it-- we were maybe-- the Jews-- were the last act in the big drama.
But there were other acts just as bad.
And just to see this-- and they may come again.
That is the worst.
You-- you can-- I see the first signs of recurrence with a combination of East Germany and West Germany.
The East Germans will not rest till they are the top dogs.
And we will have the same conditions, or the same field as we had before.
So first, the Prussians became Germany in 1971 when they crowned the-- their king emperor of Germany.
And that ended in Versailles, 50 years later.
It's now 50 years since World War II.
Something else may happen.
That's all I have to say.
Luck-- strictly luck.
At that time, there was no extermination at the camp.
The Holocaust-- it's the Final Solution.
It's that-- that's the word I was looking for.
The Final Solution was determined in Warnemunde, in 1942.
Before that time, Hitler had hoped to get the Jews out of Germany, and to be able to confiscate his-- their property, and everything else, without a blood bath.
For that was-- it didn't do him any good.
And after the outbreak of war, Hitler even tried to barter the Jews with-- with a British-- with the ally.
And it was Churchill who refused it-- to-- to take the refugees.
For he was afraid there might be some spies in them.
That's the excuse, I think-- and only after that, when the Final Solution in Warnemunde was from-- promulgated.
No, I have nightmares.
About the-- about everything.
I still have them.
No.
See, I've had enough without describing [LAUGHS] them.
And I'm afraid.
Especially tonight-- I will have a few more.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
It wouldn't do any good anyway.
I-- there's nothing I can say.
All I can say is watch out.
And that's it.
That's it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Thank you.
This is a wedding picture of my parents, Isidor Selling and Ida, nee, Laub.
It was taken 1911.
In Anspach.
It-- it was part of the stuff we brought along.
This is a picture of my father, Isidor Selling, when he was in the army, during World War I.
And did--
I think it was taken in Anspach, or wherever.
This is a picture of Hugo Laub.
This is a brother of my mother, who was in World War I-- wounded twice.
And when he finally-- his Germany didn't exist anymore, he committed suicide.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of our house in Lehrberg.
That's where we were born and where we lived till we were pushed out of Germany.
That probably was taken shortly after World War I.
This is a picture of the street where we lived.
Our house is hidden.
It was taken before 1930.
In Lehrberg.
This is a picture of my mother, and with my twin brother and me.
It was taken shortly after our birth, in 1918.
My brother's name was Leo Selling.
My mother, Ida Laub.
I'm on the left.
This was taken when we were about seven years-old, in Anspach, which is near Lehrberg.
I'm the one on the left.
My--
Over my right, that's my twin brother, Leopold.
This is my passport with which I emigrated from Germany, in 1939.
It was issued in Munich.
Of-- it's a J-- means I'm Jewish.
And as you see, the name we were all forced to take-- the Jewish name-- is in the-- in my name.
Isidor-- no, no, Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the data on my passport.
The picture was taken shortly after I came out of Dachau.
And you can see my hair is still cropped.
This is a immigration visa to England through the refugee camp, Richborough, in-- and a-- to-- the inscription says I'm only allowed to stay in the camp and cannot travel in England.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a shot of the refugee camp in-- which was taken in 1939, from a water tower nearby.
That was in-- Richborough, England-- Kitchener camp.
And it started out as a military camp in World War I, and it was-- and it was converted to a military camp in World War II.
This is a picture of my brother, Leopold Selling and me, before I went overseas from Newark, New Jersey, in 1943.
I'm in uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my two cousins, Katie Selling and Itzak Selling, whom I met up with while I was in the army.
They came from Stutthof.
They were in an extermination camp in Poland.
In-- that was in Fuerth, where they had a-- an apartment, where they could live for a while.
This is the grave of my parents, Isidor Selling and Ida Selling, and the cemetery in Anspach.
And that was a restored cemetery.
The only thing surviving were granite stones, which could not be used for building material.
Also, sandstones were destroyed.
That was taken shortly thereafter, about 1950.
This was taken in Fort Benjamin Harrison, in 1970, where I was executive officer during the summer training.
At that time, my rank was lieutenant colonel.
This is a picture of my daughter and her family.
On the left is her youngest son, Alan, and her husband, Dr.
Arnold Wise, then--
Leon, he-- it was at the time, when he received-- was inducted into the honor-- National Honor Society.
And on the right is our daughter, Barbara.
It was taken last year, in 1995.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the family of our son, Thomas.
On the left is our son, Thomas Selling.
Next to him is his-- their eldest son-- the heck--
[NON-ENGLISH].
No.
What?
Richard.
And next to him is our younger son, Matthew, and his wife, Jane Hagen.
This is my wife and helpmate, Hilde.
We have been together for 48 happy years.
But I'm not looking forward to another 48.
[LAUGHS]
50 would be enough.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS]
